label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The user is asking about the legality of a man leaving all his money to his widow's sister, with the intention to marry her next year. Hmm, I need to break this down. First, I should consider the legal term for leaving money in a will. That's called a bequest. The question is whether such a bequest is valid if the beneficiary is someone the deceased plans to marry but hasn't yet. I remember that in some jurisdictions, you can't make a valid will if you're going to get married, especially if the will was made before the marriage. But in this case, the man hasn't married his widow's sister yet. He's currently married, and his current wife is deceased. Now he's planning to marry his widow's sister (which sounds like his current wife's sister). Wait, the wording is a bit confusing. Let me parse that again. The man has a widow (his wife has died), and he plans to marry the widow's sister next year. So the sister of his ex-wife. That is, he is currently a widower, and he plans to remarry, the sister of his deceased wife. So he leaves everything to his widow's sister, whom he intends to marry next year. Is that legal? I need to recall the rules about wills and marriage. In many places, a will is automatically revoked if the person gets married after making the will, unless the will explicitly states that it's made in contemplation of marriage. So if the man had already made a will leaving everything to the sister and then marries her, the will might be invalidated unless it's clear he intended for the will to still be valid after marriage. But in this case, he hasn't married her yet. He's planning to. So the will is made before the marriage. The problem is whether a will can be made to someone you are going to marry.
